disclaimer: Seed data compiled from public sources. Every row confirmed_by_counsel=false until ratified by immigration counsel. Not legal advice.

rules.value: Eligible H-1B nonimmigrant is authorized to begin concurrent or new employment upon the filing (USCIS receipt) of a nonfrivolous H-1B petition by the new employer, or as of the requested start date, whichever is later; approval is not required before starting work.
rules.notes: Statutory since AC21 (Oct 17, 2000); codified in regulation by the AC21 final rule effective 2017-01-17 (81 FR 82398). The H-1B modernization rule (89 FR 103054, effective 2025-01-17) amended (h)(2)(i)(H) to add 'or as of the requested start date, whichever is later.' Verified against current eCFR text 2026-07-06. Confirms hypothesis: work may begin upon USCIS receipt of the petition.

rules.value: Beneficiary must (1) have been lawfully admitted to the US, (2) be the beneficiary of a nonfrivolous H-1B petition filed before the expiration of the period of authorized stay, and (3) not have been employed without authorization after such lawful admission and before the filing of the portability petition.
rules.notes: Petition must be filed before expiration of the period of stay authorized (I-94 validity or portability-based authorized stay), not merely while a visa is valid.

rules.value: Employment authorization under portability automatically continues until the new petition is adjudicated; it is NOT capped at 240 days. If the portability petition is denied, employment authorization under it ends.
rules.notes: Distinct from the 240-day rule in 8 CFR 274a.12(b)(20), which applies to timely extensions with the SAME employer.

rules.value: Successive (bridge) portability petitions are permitted: a new portability petition may be filed while a prior portability petition is pending. Each petition in the bridge must be nonfrivolous and filed before the period of authorized stay expires; if a preceding petition in the bridge is denied after the original I-94 expires, subsequent petitions generally cannot be approved for extension of stay.
rules.notes: Bridge risk: denial of an earlier link breaks status for later links unless the beneficiary otherwise maintained status.

rules.value: Per Matter of Simeio Solutions, a change in the beneficiary's place of employment to a geographical area requiring a new LCA (outside the MSA/area of intended employment on the approved petition) is a material change requiring an amended or new H-1B petition.
rules.source_citation: Matter of Simeio Solutions, LLC, 26 I&N Dec. 542 (AAO 2015); USCIS PM-602-0120 (July 21, 2015)
rules.notes: Superseded as controlling authority by regulatory codification effective 2025-01-17; the substantive requirement is unchanged. USCIS allowed a 90-day transition (until Aug 19, 2015) for moves that occurred before the decision.

rules.value: The petitioner must file an amended or new petition to reflect any material changes in the terms and conditions of employment (including a change in place of employment requiring a new LCA, or a significant change in job duties). For H-1B, the amended or new petition must be properly filed BEFORE the material change takes place.
rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 214.2(h)(2)(i)(E), as amended by 89 FR 103054 (Dec. 18, 2024), effective Jan. 17, 2025
rules.notes: H-1B modernization final rule codified the Simeio requirement and the file-before-the-change timing. Confirms hypothesis.

rules.value: Once the amended petition is filed, the H-1B worker may immediately begin working at the new worksite; the employer need not wait for adjudication of the amendment.
rules.source_citation: USCIS PM-602-0120 (July 21, 2015); INA 214(n) portability applied to amended petitions
rules.notes: If the amendment is denied, the worker must cease work at the new location; prior approved petition remains valid at the original location if otherwise maintained.

A move to a new worksite within the same MSA/area of intended employment requires no new LCA and no amended petition, but the existing LCA must still be posted at the new worksite on or before the date the worker begins there. Confirms hypothesis.

rules.value: The area within normal commuting distance of the place (address) of employment; if within an MSA or PMSA, any place within the MSA/PMSA is deemed within normal commuting distance.
rules.source_citation: 20 CFR 655.715 (definition of area of intended employment); 65 FR 80110 (Dec. 20, 2000), effective Jan. 19, 2001
rules.notes: Controls whether a worksite move triggers a new LCA and therefore an amended petition.

Placement at a worksite in an area not covered by an approved LCA shall not exceed a total of 30 workdays in a one-year period (calendar year or employer fiscal year, employer's choice, per 655.735(d)). No new LCA or amended petition required within this limit.

Up to 60 workdays in a one-year period only if the worker (1) maintains an office/workstation at the permanent worksite, (2) spends a substantial amount of time at the permanent worksite in the one-year period, and (3) has a US residence in the area of the permanent worksite, not the short-term site.

rules.value: For every day of short-term placement the employer must pay: (i) the required wage (higher of prevailing wage at the permanent worksite or the actual wage); (ii) actual cost of lodging for workdays and non-workdays; (iii) actual cost of travel, meals, and incidental expenses. On reaching the workday limit, the employer must obtain a certified LCA for the area or immediately end the placement; violating employers lose the short-term placement option for that occupation and area.
rules.notes: Short-term placement cannot be used where the employer already has a certified LCA for the occupation in that area.
Time spent in L-1 status counts toward the H-1B six-year maximum. Confirms hypothesis.
H-1B petitions are approved for up to 3 years at a time (typical pattern 3+3 to reach the 6-year maximum); validity cannot exceed the certified LCA period.

Any full day spent physically outside the US during an authorized H-1B/L period does not count against the 6-year maximum and may be recaptured at any time before the full period of admission is used. Burden is on the petitioner/beneficiary with documentary evidence (I-94 records, passport stamps, travel records); USCIS will not RFE for undocumented claimed periods. Confirms hypothesis.

An H-1B worker who has resided and been physically present outside the US for the immediately preceding year may qualify for a new full 6-year period (subject to the cap unless exempt), or may instead elect to be admitted for remaining/recaptured time.

rules.source_citation: AC21 secs. 106(a)-(b), Pub. L. 106-313, as amended by Pub. L. 107-273; 8 CFR 214.2(h)(13)(iii)(D)
rules.notes: Lengthy adjudication delay exemption: extensions beyond year 6 in up to 1-year increments, renewable until the labor certification expires or a final decision is made to approve/deny/revoke the labor certification, I-140, or adjustment/immigrant visa application. Available even if the qualifying PERM/I-140 was filed by a prior employer. Codified in regulation effective 2017-01-17.

At least 365 days must have elapsed since filing of the permanent labor certification (PERM) or the I-140, and the PERM/I-140 must remain pending or approved (not finally denied/revoked). CORRECTION to hypothesis nuance: the 2016 final rule clarified the 365 days must have elapsed as of the date the extension takes effect, not at filing - the H-1B extension petition may be filed within 6 months of the requested start date even before 365 days have elapsed.

An alien is ineligible for the 106(a) lengthy-adjudication-delay exemption if they are the beneficiary of an approved 203(b) immigrant petition and fail to file an adjustment of status application or apply for an immigrant visa within 1 year of a visa becoming immediately available for their preference category and chargeability. If visa availability retrogresses during that year, a new 1-year period is afforded when a visa again becomes available. USCIS may in its discretion excuse a failure to file due to circumstances beyond the alien's control. Confirms hypothesis (with tolling and discretionary-excuse detail). Note this bar applies to 106(a) 1-year extensions; 104(c) 3-year extensions are not subject to it.

Per-country limitation exemption: beneficiary of an approved EB-1/EB-2/EB-3 I-140 who cannot obtain the immigrant visa/adjust status solely because of per-country (or worldwide) visa limits may receive H-1B extensions of up to 3 years beyond year 6, renewable for as long as eligibility continues. Availability measured against the Visa Bulletin (final action). Available even if the I-140 was filed by a prior employer. Codified effective 2017-01-17. Confirms hypothesis.

An approved I-140 is automatically revoked on petitioner withdrawal only if the withdrawal is filed fewer than 180 days after approval AND no associated I-485 has been pending 180+ days. If withdrawn 180+ days after approval (or after an I-485 pending 180+ days), the I-140 remains valid to support AC21 104(c)/106(a) H-1B extensions and INA 204(j) portability, though it does not alone preserve a job offer for the green card. Confirms hypothesis for the extension-benefit aspect; see priority-date row for a correction.

Same 180-day structure applies when the petitioning employer's business terminates: automatic revocation only if termination occurs fewer than 180 days after approval and no I-485 has been pending 180+ days.

rules.value: The priority date of an approved EB-1/EB-2/EB-3 petition is retained for any subsequently filed EB-1/EB-2/EB-3 petition, UNLESS USCIS revokes the approval for (1) fraud or willful misrepresentation of a material fact, (2) DOL revocation of the labor certification, (3) USCIS/DOS invalidation of the labor certification, or (4) USCIS determination that approval was based on material error. Withdrawal by the employer is NOT a disqualifying ground.
rules.notes: CORRECTION to hypothesis: priority-date retention survives employer withdrawal even within 180 days of approval, because withdrawal is not among the 204.5(e)(2) exceptions. What is lost by automatic revocation within 180 days is the petition's continuing validity for AC21 extension/portability benefits, not the priority date. Earliest priority date controls when multiple approved petitions exist.

An H petition may not be filed earlier than 6 months before the date of actual need. The H-1B modernization rule (effective 2025-01-17) relocated the provision and removed the bar on APPROVAL more than 6 months out, also allowing cap-subject petitions to request start dates after October 1. Confirms hypothesis on the 6-month filing window.

A nonimmigrant (including H-1B) whose timely extension-of-stay petition with the SAME employer is pending is authorized to continue that employment for up to 240 days beginning on the date the authorized period of stay expires. Authorization ends immediately if the extension is denied before day 240. Does not apply to change-of-employer portability (see portability rows). Confirms hypothesis.

rules.value: An extension of stay may not be approved if status expired before filing, except USCIS may excuse late filing in its discretion where the delay was due to extraordinary circumstances beyond the control of the applicant or petitioner, the delay is commensurate with the circumstances, the alien has not otherwise violated nonimmigrant status, and remains a bona fide nonimmigrant.
rules.notes: Longstanding provision; paragraph designations amended over time.

rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 214.1(c)(5), added by 89 FR 103054, effective Jan. 17, 2025
rules.notes: When adjudicating an I-129 involving the same parties and same underlying facts, USCIS gives deference to its prior eligibility determination unless there was a material error, a material change in circumstances or eligibility requirements, or new material adverse information. Applies to extensions, amendments, change of status, and consular-notification petitions.

H-1B (also E-1, E-2, E-3, H-1B1, L-1, O-1, TN) workers are not considered out of status solely due to cessation of employment for up to 60 consecutive days or until the end of the authorized validity period, whichever is shorter, once per authorized validity period. No work permitted during the grace period; a portability petition filed within it enables a new start.

rules.value: October 1 of the fiscal year for which H-1B status is requested
rules.notes: Old rule: F-1 status and OPT work authorization of a cap-gap-eligible student extended only until Oct 1. Superseded by the H-1B modernization rule effective 2025-01-17, first applied in the FY2026 cap season.

rules.value: April 1 of the fiscal year for which H-1B status is requested (i.e., April 1 following the requested October 1 start date), or until the validity start date of the approved petition, whichever is earlier; terminates automatically on rejection, denial, revocation, or withdrawal of the petition or the change-of-status request
rules.notes: CORRECTED 2026-07-06: regulation text reads 'until April 1 of the fiscal year for which such H-1B status is being requested' (e.g., FY2026 petitions extend to April 1, 2026), not 'April 1 of the calendar year following the fiscal year.' Verified against current 8 CFR 214.2(f)(5)(vi)(A) via eCFR. F-1 students with timely filed, nonfrivolous cap-subject change-of-status H-1B petitions get automatic extension of F-1 duration of status and any employment authorization (under 8 CFR 274a.12(c)(3)(i)(B) or (C)) to as late as April 1, closing the gap caused by petitions still pending after Oct 1.

I-129 fee for H-1B classification: $780 paper filing ($730 online filing); reduced to $460 for nonprofits and small employers (25 or fewer full-time-equivalent employees). Applies to new petitions, transfers, amendments, and extensions. VERIFIED 2026-07-06 against current G-1055 (Edition 05/29/26): amounts unchanged since the April 1, 2024 fee rule (89 FR 6194).

$600 per I-129/I-140 filing; $300 for small employers (25 or fewer FTEs); $0 for nonprofits. VERIFIED 2026-07-06 against current G-1055 (Edition 05/29/26): amounts unchanged since the April 1, 2024 fee rule (89 FR 6194).

rules.source_citation: INA 214(c)(9); Pub. L. 108-447; USCIS Fee Schedule (G-1055)
rules.notes: $1,500, reduced to $750 for employers with 25 or fewer full-time employees. Exempt: institutions of higher education, related/affiliated nonprofits, nonprofit and governmental research organizations, primary/secondary schools, certain curriculum-related nonprofits. Payable on initial petition and first extension by an employer; not on second or subsequent extensions by the same employer or amendments without an extension of stay.

rules.source_citation: INA 214(c)(12); Pub. L. 108-447; USCIS Fee Schedule (G-1055)
rules.notes: $500, payable on initial H-1B grants and change-of-employer (transfer) petitions; not required for extensions with the same employer.

rules.source_citation: Pub. L. 114-113, Div. O, Title IV, sec. 402
rules.notes: $4,000 additional fee for petitioners with 50 or more US employees where more than 50 percent are in H-1B, L-1A, or L-1B status; applies to initial and change-of-employer H-1B petitions ($4,500 L-1 analog). Sunset VERIFIED 2026-07-06: current USCIS Fee Schedule G-1055 (Edition 05/29/26) states the fee applies where the petition is filed before October 1, 2027, i.e., in effect through Sept. 30, 2027; original Pub. L. 114-113 sunset of Sept. 30, 2025 was extended to Sept. 30, 2027 by Pub. L. 115-123 (Bipartisan Budget Act of 2018), sec. 30203.

rules.source_citation: USCIS premium processing fee for Form I-129 H-1B (Feb. 26, 2024 - Feb. 28, 2026)
rules.notes: Superseded: inflation adjustment effective 2026-03-01 raised the I-129 premium processing fee.

rules.source_citation: Adjustment to Premium Processing Fees, 91 FR (Jan. 12, 2026), effective Mar. 1, 2026
rules.notes: Requests postmarked on or after 2026-03-01 must include $2,965 for I-129 H-1B. Premium processing clock for H-1B I-129: 15 business days.
15 business days (not calendar days) for I-129 H-1B classifications; clock restarts on RFE response.

rules.source_citation: Presidential Proclamation 'Restriction on Entry of Certain Nonimmigrant Workers' (Sept. 19, 2025); USCIS H-1B FAQ
rules.notes: Applies to NEW H-1B petitions filed at or after 12:01 a.m. EDT Sept. 21, 2025 for beneficiaries OUTSIDE the US who do not hold a valid H-1B visa. Does NOT apply to: extensions, amendments, or changes of status granted for beneficiaries inside the US (including their later visa applications on the approved petition); holders of H-1B visas issued before 2025-09-21; petitions filed before 2025-09-21. National-interest exceptions via DHS (H1BExceptions@hq.dhs.gov). LITIGATION STATUS as of 2026-07-06 (caption CORRECTED from 'v. Noem'): D. Mass. vacated the fee 2026-06-08 in State of California v. Mullin, No. 1:25-cv-13829 (D. Mass.), as an unlawful tax/APA violation; on 2026-06-12 the court denied a stay pending appeal but granted an administrative stay of its vacatur conditioned on the government moving in the First Circuit, which it did 2026-06-18 (State of California v. Mullin, 1st Cir. No. 26-1699). No First Circuit ruling located as of 2026-07-06. Separately, D.D.C. upheld the fee (Chamber of Commerce v. DHS, No. 1:25-cv-03675, summary judgment for government), on appeal to the D.C. Circuit (No. 25-5473, argued March 2026, pending). The fee REMAINS IN EFFECT and payable as of 2026-07-06 while the administrative stay is in place; it is still listed as an additional I-129 H-1B fee in G-1055 (Edition 05/29/26). Renamed from h1b-proclamation-100k-payment to avoid rule_id collision with the fee-registry row in uscis_fees.json.

A worker previously counted against the H-1B cap within the past 6 years is not counted again; change-of-employer petitions for cap-counted workers may be filed year-round without registration/lottery.
